title: CI/CD Pipeline for Microservices + Web + Mobile — System Design
description: Design the delivery platform that takes a commit and ships it safely to production: build and test a fleet of microservices and a modern web frontend, plus iOS and Android apps; produce immutable, signed artifacts; promote them through environments with canary/blue-green rollouts; and auto-rollback on bad signals. The themes are reproducible builds, fan-out across heterogeneous targets, progressive delivery, and the very different release cadence of mobile (store review + staged rollout) versus server/web (deploy on demand).

nodes.label: Developer
nodes.description: Engineers who push commits and open pull requests.

nodes.label: Git Host (VCS)
nodes.description: GitHub/GitLab: source of truth for code; emits push/PR webhooks.

nodes.label: Pipeline Trigger
nodes.description: Receives VCS webhooks and starts the matching pipeline for the changed services.

nodes.label: CI/CD Orchestrator
nodes.description: Resolves the pipeline DAG, schedules stages, tracks run state, fans out to runners.

nodes.label: Build Runner Pool
nodes.description: Ephemeral build/test agents (Linux containers + macOS hosts for iOS) that execute jobs.

nodes.label: Build Cache
nodes.description: Dependency + layer + test cache keyed by lockfile/inputs to skip redundant work.

nodes.label: Artifact Registry
nodes.description: Stores immutable build outputs: container images, web bundles, signed mobile binaries.

nodes.label: Signing / Secrets
nodes.description: Code-signing (iOS certs/provisioning, Android keystore) and artifact signing + attestations; pulls from a secrets vault.

nodes.label: Deployment Controller
nodes.description: Promotes an artifact version into an environment (GitOps/rollout) and drives traffic shifting.

nodes.label: Microservice Runtime (K8s)
nodes.description: The cluster running the microservices + web app behind a service mesh / ingress.

nodes.label: Web CDN / Edge
nodes.description: Serves the versioned web frontend bundle to browsers.

nodes.label: App Stores
nodes.description: Apple App Store Connect + Google Play: review, staged rollout, and distribution of mobile builds.

nodes.label: Observability + Gates
nodes.description: Metrics/logs/traces, deploy markers, and SLO error-budget gates that auto-rollback bad releases.

nodes.label: Pipeline State Store
nodes.description: Durable run history, stage status, approvals, and the version->environment ledger.

functional: On every push/PR, build and test only the affected services (web, backend, mobile).
Produce immutable, versioned, signed artifacts: container images, web bundles, and mobile binaries (.ipa / .aab).
Promote a specific artifact version through environments (dev -> staging -> prod) with approvals.
Deploy services/web with progressive strategies (canary, blue-green) and one-click rollback.
Release mobile apps through App Store Connect and Google Play with staged rollout.
Show every run's status, logs, and the version-to-environment history.
nonFunctional: Fast feedback: PR pipeline p50 under ~15 minutes despite a large monorepo.
Reproducible & hermetic builds — same inputs produce the same artifact.
Safe by default: no bad version reaches 100% of users without a gate.
Secure: signing keys and secrets never leak into build logs or artifacts.
Auditable: who shipped what version where, and when, is recoverable.
Self-healing: a degraded release auto-rolls-back on SLO breach.

capacity.label: Commits / day
capacity.note: Spiky around work hours; size for the peak, ~1 run/sec, not the daily mean.

capacity.label: Affected targets / PR
capacity.note: A wide shared-lib change fans out; full monorepo (~40 svc + web) is the worst case.

capacity.label: Jobs & duration
capacity.note: lint, build, unit, integration, package per target.

capacity.label: Linux runner pool
capacity.value: ~6 targets x 8 jobs x ~5 min / 2-min queue SLO ≈ 120 warm vCPU-slots, autoscaled
capacity.note: Keep a warm pool to hide ~30-60s cold-start; burst higher at peak.

capacity.label: macOS runner pool (iOS)
capacity.note: Xcode needs real macOS HW/VMs; iOS jobs queue here, not on Linux.

capacity.label: Build cache
capacity.note: Content-keyed deps/layers/test caches; eviction window ~7-14 days; remote-cache bandwidth matters.

capacity.label: Artifact + log retention
capacity.note: Images, web bundles, .ipa/.aab, SBOMs, attestations, test reports, deploy markers.

capacity.label: Pipeline latency
capacity.note: Cache + parallel fan-out; queue wait target < 2 min.

api.description: VCS push/PR event; computes affected targets and starts a run. Idempotency-Key dedupes retried deliveries so one event makes one run.
api.request: { repo, ref, commit, changedPaths[] }  + Idempotency-Key header

api.description: Run status, per-stage/job results, attempts, queue/run times, and produced artifact versions.
Stream or fetch logs for a stage/job attempt by its log pointer.
Rerun a single failed/flaky job as a new attempt without restarting the whole run.
Cancel a queued/running run; in-flight jobs drain and runners are released.
Promote an artifact version into an environment with a strategy. Verifies signature/provenance/policy before admitting; idempotent on retry.
Approve (or reject) a promotion gated on policy; records actor, policy, and expiry.
Pause or resume an in-flight rollout (pass action). Holds the current traffic split.
Roll back an environment to the previous known-good version. Idempotent; drives the rollback state machine.
Ledger of versions deployed to an environment: who approved, when, gate results, current live + previous-good.
Submit a signed mobile binary to a store track. Async: returns a release record polled for review/rollout state. Idempotent so a retry never double-submits.
Current mobile release state: review status, track, staged rollout %, crash-gate status.
Halt a staged rollout (or resume/roll-back-on-Play); records the action for forward-fix linkage.

dataModel.name: run_stages
dataModel.note: DAG stage within a run (lint, build, test, package).

dataModel.name: jobs
dataModel.note: Unit of work for one target on one runner type.

dataModel.name: job_attempts
dataModel.note: Each (re)try of a job; makes retries first-class.

dataModel.name: environment_versions
dataModel.note: Source of truth for what is live per environment.

dataModel.name: deployment_steps
dataModel.note: Rollout state machine for one promotion.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedupe retried webhooks, run starts, deploys, rollbacks, store submissions.

patterns.name: Pipeline as DAG
patterns.what: Model the pipeline as a directed acyclic graph of stages/jobs with explicit dependencies, fanned out across runners.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever build/test/deploy steps have dependencies and parallelism.

patterns.name: Affected-only builds
patterns.what: Compute the dependency graph and rebuild/test only targets affected by the diff.
patterns.whenToUse: Large monorepos where rebuilding everything is too slow.

patterns.name: Immutable, signed artifacts
patterns.what: Build once into a content-addressed, signed artifact; promote the same bytes through environments.
patterns.whenToUse: Always — prevents drift between what was tested and what ships.

patterns.name: Progressive delivery
patterns.what: Shift traffic gradually (canary/blue-green) with automated gates instead of a big-bang deploy.
patterns.whenToUse: Production deploys where blast radius must be bounded.

patterns.name: GitOps
patterns.what: Declare desired environment state in Git; a controller reconciles the cluster to match.

patterns.name: Idempotent control plane
patterns.what: Key mutating operations (webhook, run start, deploy, rollback, store submission) so retries are dedup'd instead of duplicated.
patterns.whenToUse: Any at-least-once event source (VCS webhooks, retried API calls).

patterns.name: Deploy-time admission policy
patterns.what: Re-verify signature, provenance, scan result, and policy at deploy time; a registry entry alone is never deployable.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever supply-chain integrity must hold from source to runtime.

patterns.name: Rollout state machine
patterns.what: Model a rollout as explicit states (pending_approval, ramping, paused, aborted, live, rollback_in_progress, rolled_back) with manual override and post-rollback verification.
patterns.whenToUse: Progressive delivery that must pause, abort, and resume safely.

patterns.name: Expand / contract migration
patterns.what: Phase schema changes so old and new app versions stay compatible during a canary.
patterns.whenToUse: Stateful changes shipped behind progressive delivery.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Script on a Build Box (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works: a single shell script on one always-on build server that, on every commit, builds everything, runs the whole test suite, and copies outputs to prod.

It's correct for a tiny app, and it exposes every problem we'll fix: it rebuilds all 40 services even for a one-line web change (slow), it's a single serial machine (no parallelism, a queue forms), there's no macOS host so iOS can't build, secrets sit in the script, and 'deploy' is a copy with no canary or rollback.

Put a number on it: 40 services × ~8 min each serially is ~5 hours per commit — at 500 commits/day (peak ~4×) the queue blows past the workday almost immediately. Parallelism, affected-only builds, and caching all exist to kill that number.
steps.decisionPrompt: On a 500-commit day, what breaks first: build time, the single machine, or the unsafe deploy?
whyNow: The naive baseline makes the four real problems obvious: speed, parallelism, heterogeneous targets, and safe release.

recap.after: Every commit builds-tests-ships via one script on one box.
recap.newRisk: Serial, rebuilds-everything, no iOS host, secrets in the open, no safe rollout.

traps.trap: Shipping the build-box script as 'good enough CI/CD'.
traps.why: It's serial, non-reproducible, can't build iOS, and deploys with no rollback path.
traps.instead: Model a real pipeline DAG with ephemeral runners and progressive delivery.

steps.title: 2. Model the Pipeline as a DAG on Ephemeral Runners
description: Replace the script with an orchestrator that turns each run into a DAG of jobs (lint, build, unit, integration, package) and dispatches them to a pool of ephemeral, single-use runners.

Ephemeral runners give hermetic, reproducible builds (clean state every time) and let independent jobs run in parallel. The orchestrator persists run → stage → job → attempt state with runner leases, a retry policy, queue/start/end times, and log pointers, so a crashed run resumes the unfinished jobs or fails cleanly, a flaky job reruns as a new attempt, and a lost runner's lease expires and reschedules.
Heterogeneous targets need heterogeneous runners: Linux containers for backend/web, dedicated macOS hosts for iOS (Xcode), and Android runs on Linux.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why ephemeral single-use runners instead of long-lived agents?

options.name: Kubernetes-native DAG runner (default)
options.description: A controller (Tekton/Argo-style) compiles each run into a DAG and schedules every job as an ephemeral pod, scaling runners with the cluster.
pros: Hermetic by construction: one fresh pod per job.
Elastic — autoscale runners with cluster capacity.
DAG dependencies and retries are first-class.
cons: You operate the runner fleet and macOS hosts (iOS pods can't run on Linux).

view.caption: A controller compiles the run into a DAG and dispatches each job to an ephemeral runner pod.

options.name: Jenkins-style central controller
options.description: A single long-lived controller with a pool of (often persistent) agents executes pipeline scripts.
pros: Simple mental model for small teams.
cons: Persistent agents drift from hermetic; 'works on my agent' flakiness.
Controller is a scaling + availability bottleneck.
Scripted pipelines mix orchestration with build logic.

view.caption: A central controller drives a pool of persistent agents — simple but harder to keep hermetic and to scale.

options.name: Managed CI (CodeBuild / Cloud Build / Azure Pipelines)
options.description: Hand the runner fleet to a cloud CI service; you author pipelines, the provider runs the compute.
pros: No runner-fleet ops; pay per build-minute.
Ephemeral, hermetic runners out of the box.
cons: Constrained build environments — macOS/iOS support is limited and costly.
Less control over caching, network, and custom hardware.
Per-minute cost scales with a large monorepo.

view.caption: A managed CI service owns the runner fleet; you only author the pipeline.

concepts.definition: A build that depends only on declared inputs, isolated from ambient machine state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Same inputs -> same artifact; eliminates 'works on my runner' flakiness.
concepts.example: A fresh container per job with a pinned toolchain and lockfile.

concepts.definition: A client-supplied key that dedupes a mutating request so retries don't create duplicates.
concepts.whyItMatters: VCS webhooks deliver at-least-once; without it, one push can spawn duplicate runs (and later, duplicate deploys/submissions).
concepts.example: POST /webhooks/vcs with Idempotency-Key=<delivery-id> returns the existing run on retry.

concepts.definition: Isolation that prevents untrusted PR code (e.g. from a fork) from accessing secrets or writing shared caches.
concepts.whyItMatters: A malicious PR could otherwise exfiltrate signing keys or poison the cache for everyone.
concepts.example: Fork PRs run on a restricted pool with no secrets and read-only cache.

whyNow: A DAG + ephemeral runners is the foundation everything else (cache, artifacts, deploy) hangs off.

recap.after: Parallel DAG of jobs on clean ephemeral runners.
recap.newRisk: Cold runners redo dependency installs/compiles every time — still slow.

steps.title: 3. Fast Feedback: Affected-Only Builds + Caching
description: A clean runner every time is reproducible but slow: it re-downloads dependencies and recompiles unchanged code. Two levers cut PR latency to the target.
First, affected-only builds: compute the dependency graph and run jobs only for targets the diff touches — a web-only change shouldn't build 40 backend services or the mobile apps.
Second, a shared build cache keyed by content (lockfile hash, source hash): restore dependency caches, compiler/test caches, and container layers so unchanged work is skipped while staying hermetic.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you key the cache so it's reused safely but never returns stale outputs?

concepts.definition: The set of build targets reachable from changed files through the dependency graph.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds work to the change, the single biggest monorepo speedup.
concepts.example: Touching a shared auth lib rebuilds its dependents; touching web CSS does not.

whyNow: Speed is a hard requirement; caching/affected-only is the direct lever after step 2 made builds parallel.

deepDives.title: Remote-cache correctness (don't let it poison builds)
points: Key = hash of all declared inputs: source files, lockfiles, toolchain version, build flags, and platform — not branch or time.
Namespace the cache and make untrusted-PR builds read-only: a fork's job may read trusted entries but must not write, or it can poison main's cache.
Always have a correctness fallback: a cache miss rebuilds from source; a cache hit must be byte-identical to a fresh build (hermetic) or the key is wrong.
Observe hit rate per target; a sudden drop signals a key that's too specific (no reuse) or a non-hermetic input leaking in.

traps.trap: Caching on a branch name or timestamp.
traps.why: Returns stale artifacts or never hits; both defeat the purpose.
traps.instead: Key on content hashes of inputs (lockfiles, sources, toolchain version).

traps.trap: Letting untrusted fork PRs write to the shared cache.
traps.why: A malicious PR can seed a poisoned entry that later trusted builds restore.
traps.instead: Read-only cache for untrusted PRs; only trusted (post-merge) jobs write.

followUps: Add a merge queue / speculative pre-merge testing so main stays green as concurrency grows.
Remote build execution + test selection for when the affected graph itself gets huge.

recap.after: Only affected targets build; content-keyed caches skip redundant work (with poisoning guards).
recap.newRisk: Outputs are still mutable/untracked — no single source of truth for 'what shipped'.

steps.title: 4. Immutable, Signed Artifacts (build once, promote everywhere)
description: Stop rebuilding per environment. Each successful run produces immutable, content-addressed artifacts pushed to a registry: container images for services, a versioned web bundle, and mobile binaries (.aab for Android, .ipa for iOS).

Mobile artifacts must be code-signed: iOS needs an Apple certificate + provisioning profile; Android needs the upload/app-signing keystore. Signing material and other secrets come from a vault and are injected at build time, never written to logs or baked into source.
Sign artifacts and emit a provenance attestation (what commit, which inputs) so the same tested bytes are promoted through every environment — no drift between staging and prod.

Attach an SBOM and a vulnerability-scan result to each artifact. A registry entry alone must never be deployable: at deploy time the controller re-verifies the signature, provenance for the expected commit, passing checks, no critical CVEs, and a policy approval before it changes any environment's desired state.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why build the artifact once and promote it, instead of rebuilding for staging and prod separately?

concepts.definition: Signed metadata recording how an artifact was built (commit, inputs, builder).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you verify what you deploy is what you tested, and trace supply-chain risk.
concepts.example: An in-toto/SLSA attestation attached to the image digest.

concepts.definition: A machine-readable inventory of every dependency baked into an artifact.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets a policy gate block deploys with critical CVEs and answer 'are we exposed to this CVE?' instantly.
concepts.example: A CycloneDX/SPDX SBOM stored alongside the image digest, refreshed on each build.

whyNow: Immutable artifacts are the contract the deploy stage promotes — must exist before progressive delivery.

deepDives.title: Supply chain, end to end — admission is the chokepoint
points: Runner identity: jobs authenticate to the cloud/registry with short-lived OIDC tokens, not long-lived static keys.
Scan + SBOM at build time: dependency and container scanning produce a scan result and SBOM attached to the artifact.
Signed-commit / branch-protection signals feed the provenance: 'built from a protected branch at commit X'.
Deploy-time admission gate (between registry and deployer) re-verifies digest, signature, provenance for the approved run, scan pass, and policy — only then does the controller change desired state.
If the attestation is missing or the signing key was rotated mid-release, admission fails closed and the rollout is blocked, not silently allowed.

traps.trap: Rebuilding the artifact for each environment.
traps.why: The prod build can differ from what passed in staging, hiding bugs until release.
traps.instead: Build once; promote the identical signed digest.

traps.trap: Putting signing keys or tokens in environment variables visible in logs.
traps.why: Leaked signing keys let attackers ship malicious app updates.
traps.instead: Pull from a vault with short-lived, scoped credentials; mask in logs.

traps.trap: Treating a registry push as sufficient to deploy.
traps.why: Anything that lands in the registry could ship, including an unscanned or unsigned image.
traps.instead: Require deploy-time admission: signature + provenance + scan pass + policy before promotion.

failureDrills.scenario: Attestation is missing, or the signing key was rotated mid-release so the digest no longer verifies.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Admission fails closed — the deploy is blocked, not silently allowed; the run is flagged for re-sign/re-attest.
failureDrills.mitigation: Verify signature + provenance at admission, version signing keys, and overlap old/new keys during rotation.

recap.after: One signed, content-addressed artifact per run, with SBOM + scan, admitted only after verification.
recap.newRisk: Pushing that artifact straight to 100% of prod is still a big-bang deploy.

steps.title: 5. Progressive Delivery for Services + Web (canary / blue-green, GitOps)
description: Promotion is a separate concern from build. A deployment controller takes an artifact version and rolls it into an environment using a progressive strategy instead of replacing everything at once.

Canary: route a small slice of traffic to the new version, watch metrics, then ramp. Blue-green: stand up the new version alongside the old and flip the router once healthy. The web bundle ships as immutable, versioned assets to the CDN and is activated by flipping a single active-version pointer (e.g. index.html / a manifest), with old asset versions kept and cache-busting on the bundle hash so cached clients stay consistent.

Web releases must stay compatible with the backend: a bundle that calls an API not yet live for all users will break. Gate the active-pointer flip on the API being deployed first, or feature-flag the new client path.

Desired state is declared in Git (GitOps): the controller reconciles the cluster to match, so every change is reviewed, audited, and revertible by reverting a commit. Approvals are a durable, gated workflow — recorded with actor, policy, and expiry — before promotion into staging and prod.
steps.decisionPrompt: When is blue-green better than canary, and when does canary win?

options.name: Canary (default)
options.description: Route a small % of live traffic to the new version, compare its golden signals to stable, then ramp 5% → 25% → 100% or abort.
pros: Smallest blast radius — a bad version hurts a fraction of users.
Real production traffic exercises the new version.
Natural place to attach automated gates.
cons: Needs enough traffic for signals to be significant.
Both versions run at once — must be forward/backward compatible (schema, APIs).

view.caption: Canary shifts a small traffic slice to the new version and ramps only if signals stay healthy.

options.name: Blue-green
options.description: Stand up the full new version (green) beside the old (blue) and flip the router all at once when green is healthy.
pros: Instant cutover and instant rollback (flip back to blue).
Good when you can't split traffic by percentage or need an atomic switch.
cons: All-or-nothing — no gradual blast-radius bounding, so the first bad request hits everyone.

view.caption: Blue-green runs both versions and flips the router atomically once the new one is healthy.

options.name: Rolling update
options.description: Replace instances a few at a time in place until the whole fleet runs the new version.
pros: Built into Kubernetes Deployments — simplest to operate.
cons: Mixed-version window with no traffic control or fast abort.
Rollback means rolling forward again — slower than a traffic flip.

view.caption: Rolling update swaps instances in batches — no extra capacity, but a mixed-version window and slow abort.

deepDives.title: GitOps vs imperative deploy APIs
points: Imperative: a deploy API tells the cluster 'apply this now'. Fast and direct, but the live state can drift from any record and rollback is a runbook.
GitOps: desired state lives in a Git repo and a controller continuously reconciles the cluster to it. The repo is the audit log; rollback is reverting a commit; drift self-heals.
Trade-off: GitOps adds a reconcile loop and a second repo to manage, and emergency changes still need a fast path; imperative is simpler for one-off or break-glass actions.
Common shape: GitOps for steady-state desired version + an imperative pause/abort/rollback path for the live rollout state machine.

concepts.definition: Send a small % of traffic to the new version, compare health, then ramp or abort.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds blast radius — a bad version hurts 1% of users, not everyone.

whyNow: With immutable artifacts in hand, safe promotion is the next risk to retire before automating rollback.

recap.after: Canary/blue-green promotion of immutable versions via GitOps.
recap.newRisk: Ramp decisions are still manual — humans watching dashboards miss things.

steps.title: 6. Automated Gates + Rollback (close the loop with observability)
description: Make the canary self-driving. Wire the deployment controller to observability: every rollout step compares the canary's golden signals (error rate, latency, saturation) and the service's SLO error budget against the stable version.

If a gate fails, the controller automatically aborts the ramp and rolls back to the previous known-good version — instantly for services/web because the old version is still running and you just shift traffic back. Deploy markers correlate the regression with the exact version.
This closes the loop: bad releases are detected and reverted without a human in the critical path, satisfying the 'no bad version reaches everyone' requirement.

Rollout is a state machine, not a single shift: pending_approval → ramping → (paused) → live, or → aborted → rollback_in_progress → rolled_back, with manual override and a post-rollback verification step. A gate needs explicit inputs — which signal, what window, what threshold, and a minimum sample size — so 'metrics healthy' can't hide a noisy or under-sampled canary.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which signals can a gate trust within minutes, and which are too slow to gate on?

concepts.definition: An automated check that halts/rolls back a rollout when SLO burn exceeds a threshold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns reliability targets into an enforceable release control.
concepts.example: Abort if canary 5xx rate is 3x the stable baseline over 2 minutes.

concepts.definition: Roll out a schema change in compatible phases: add the new field (expand), deploy code that writes both, backfill, then remove the old field later (contract).
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps old and new app versions both working during a canary, so a traffic-shift rollback stays safe.
concepts.example: Add nullable column → dual-write → backfill → switch reads → drop old column in a later release.

whyNow: Automated rollback is the payoff: it makes the progressive delivery from step 5 safe without manual babysitting.

deepDives.title: Migrations inside a rollout (expand/contract)
points: Never couple a destructive schema change to the same release that depends on it — a canary rollback can't undo a dropped column.
Expand: deploy a backward-compatible schema (add nullable columns/tables) first, independent of app code.
Migrate: deploy the app that dual-writes, backfill data in the background, then switch reads to the new shape.
Contract: only after the old version is fully gone and verified, remove old fields in a later release.
During the canary both versions read/write a schema both understand, so a traffic shift back is always safe.

deepDives.title: Gate policy inputs (what 'healthy' must mean)
points: Signals: error rate, latency (p95/p99), saturation, plus business guardrails (conversion, revenue) and synthetic checks.
For mobile: crash-free sessions and ANR rate feed the same gate model.
Each gate names a window and threshold (e.g. 5xx > 3x stable over 2 min) AND a minimum sample size, so low-traffic canaries don't pass or fail on noise.
Slow signals (weekly retention, billing) can't gate a minutes-long canary — guard those with flags and post-release monitoring instead.

failureDrills.scenario: New version raises p99 latency 4x on the canary.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Gate trips within minutes; ramp aborts and traffic returns to stable.
failureDrills.mitigation: Keep old version warm during canary so rollback is a traffic shift, not a redeploy.

failureDrills.scenario: Canary metric looks bad but it's noise — the canary gets too little traffic for the signal to be significant.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The gate withholds a verdict until the minimum sample size is met instead of flapping abort/ramp.
failureDrills.mitigation: Require a minimum request count per gate; widen the window or raise the canary % for low-traffic services.

failureDrills.scenario: Canary ships a destructive migration (drops a column the stable version still reads).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Stable starts erroring; a traffic-shift rollback does NOT fix it because the schema is already gone.
failureDrills.mitigation: Use expand/contract — never drop in the same release; the canary only ever runs against a compatible schema.

failureDrills.scenario: A new web bundle calls a backend API not yet available to all users.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients on the new bundle get errors against old backends in the mixed-version window.
failureDrills.mitigation: Deploy the backend first, gate the active-pointer flip on it, or feature-flag the new client path.

traps.trap: Auto-rolling-back a stateful migration the same way as a stateless service.
traps.why: A schema change can't always be undone by shifting traffic.
traps.instead: Use expand/contract migrations so old and new versions both work during the canary.

traps.trap: Rolling back to an old version that wasn't kept warm or compatible.
traps.why: If the previous-good version was scaled to zero or its dependencies moved, the 'rollback' is itself a risky cold deploy.
traps.instead: Keep previous-good warm and compatible; treat rollback as a first-class, tested path with post-rollback verification.

recap.after: Gated, auto-rollback rollouts driven by SLOs.
recap.newRisk: Mobile can't behave like this — store review and uninstall-less rollback need their own path.

steps.title: 7. Mobile Release Pipeline (store review + staged rollout)
description: Mobile breaks the server model: you can't shift traffic, users update on their own schedule, and Apple/Google gate releases with review. The pipeline reflects this different cadence.

CI signs the binaries (iOS .ipa on a macOS runner, Android .aab) and the deployer uploads to App Store Connect and Google Play. Internal/TestFlight and Play internal tracks get the build first for QA, then it's submitted for review.

Release safely with staged rollout — Play's percentage rollout and phased App Store release expose the build to a growing fraction of users. There is no instant rollback: you can halt the rollout and ship a forward-fix or, on Play, roll back to a prior version for users who haven't updated. Feature flags + a server-driven kill switch are the real 'rollback' for code already on devices.

The store workflow is asynchronous and stateful, so the pipeline tracks it rather than controlling it: a release moves through internal/TestFlight or Play internal → closed/open → production tracks, and a review status (pending → in_review → approved/rejected) the pipeline learns via store callbacks or polling. Crash-free sessions and adoption metrics feed a crash gate that can halt the staged rollout, just like SLO gates do for services.

Version skew is a first-class concern: because users update on their own schedule, the backend must support old clients for weeks. Each release records a min_backend_version and the kill switches it depends on, and a compatibility policy defines how long old clients are supported.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why can't mobile use the same instant traffic-shift rollback as the services?

concepts.definition: Release a mobile build to a growing % of users (e.g. 1% -> 10% -> 100%) so crashes surface early.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds blast radius despite no traffic-shifting and no forced update.
concepts.example: Play 10% rollout, watch crash-free sessions, then ramp.

concepts.definition: Stores expose tracks (TestFlight/internal → closed/open → production) and a review status the pipeline must poll or receive as a callback, not control directly.
concepts.whyItMatters: The platform tracks an async, externally-owned state machine instead of pretending it can deploy like Kubernetes.
concepts.example: A mobile_releases row holds track, store_submission_id, review_status, and rollout_pct.

concepts.definition: The spread of client versions in the wild because users update on their own schedule (or never).
concepts.whyItMatters: The backend must keep supporting old clients for weeks; new features need flags and a min_backend_version.
concepts.example: Backend honors a min_backend_version policy and keeps deprecated endpoints until adoption crosses a threshold.

whyNow: Mobile is the final piece: same artifact/quality gates, fundamentally different release mechanics.

messages.label: halt rollout; forward-fix / flag kill switch

traps.trap: Assuming you can 'roll back' a shipped mobile build like a server.
traps.why: Users who updated keep the bad binary; stores don't force-downgrade.
traps.instead: Halt the staged rollout, use feature flags / server kill switches, and ship a fast forward-fix.

traps.trap: Shipping a backend change that breaks old mobile clients.
traps.why: Many users are on prior versions for weeks; a breaking API change is an outage for them.
traps.instead: Maintain backward-compatible APIs behind a min_backend_version policy until old-client adoption drops.

failureDrills.scenario: A backend change must support mobile clients that won't update for weeks.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Old clients keep working; the new behavior is gated by flag and a min_backend_version, not forced on everyone.
failureDrills.mitigation: Additive/compatible API changes, version negotiation, and a deprecation window tied to adoption metrics.

recap.after: Mobile released via store review + staged rollout with crash gates and flag-based kill switches; release state tracked async.
recap.newRisk: Coordinating a feature that spans server + web + mobile releases needs flagging discipline.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — CI/CD Pipeline
finalDesign.description: A commit triggers an orchestrated DAG run on ephemeral runners that build only affected targets with shared, content-keyed caching, producing immutable signed artifacts (images, web bundle, mobile binaries) — each with an SBOM, scan result, and provenance attestation — in a registry. The Pipeline State Store holds run/stage/job state, approvals, environment_versions (the source of truth for what is live), and an audit log. A deployment controller promotes a verified artifact only after a deploy-time admission check (signature + provenance + scan + policy) and a durable approval, using canary/blue-green/rolling for services and a versioned CDN pointer for web, driven by GitOps with an imperative pause/abort/rollback path. Observability gates auto-rollback degraded canaries via the rollout state machine. Mobile takes a parallel, asynchronous path: signed binaries go to store tracks for review and staged rollout, with crash gates and feature-flag kill switches; feature flags (external to the CI/CD platform) are required for safe cross-client and mobile releases under version skew.

tradeoff: Managed CI removes runner-fleet ops but constrains custom build environments (notably macOS for iOS).
Self-hosted gives full control of retention/signing; managed registries integrate signing + scanning out of the box.
Argo/Flux are Kubernetes-native and auditable via Git; managed deploy services are simpler but less flexible on custom gates.
makesIrrelevant: A good GitOps controller removes most bespoke 'deploy scripts' and manual rollback runbooks.

tradeoff: iOS requires real macOS hardware/VMs for Xcode; managed mobile CI rents that but at higher per-minute cost.
Self-hosted scanners/policy give full control and offline operation; managed scanning + binary-authorization wire admission into the registry and deployer for you.
makesIrrelevant: Binary authorization / admission policy removes ad-hoc 'is this image safe to deploy?' checks scattered across deploy scripts.

tradeoff: Flags are external to the CI/CD platform but a hard dependency for safe mobile and cross-client releases; build vs buy trades control for a managed targeting/SDK story.
The real 'rollback' for code already on user devices — there is no traffic shift for an installed app.
Centralized vaults give short-lived scoped creds and audit logs; file-based secrets are simpler but riskier.

functional.requirement: Build/test affected targets on each change
functional.how: DAG run on ephemeral runners with an affected-target graph.
Content-addressed images/bundles/binaries signed and stored in the registry.
Deployment controller promotes the same digest via GitOps with approvals.
Canary/blue-green with auto-rollback on gate failure.
Signed binaries uploaded to App Store Connect / Play with staged rollout.
Pipeline state store records runs/stages/jobs/attempts with log pointers, plus environment_versions and an audit log as the source of truth for what's live.

nonFunctional.how: Affected-only builds + content-keyed caching + parallel fan-out.
Clean ephemeral runners with pinned toolchains and locked inputs.
No version reaches 100% without passing progressive-delivery gates.
Vault-issued short-lived creds; keys never logged or baked in; SBOM + scan + deploy-time admission verify the supply chain end to end.
Durable approvals (actor/policy/expiry), an append-only audit log, environment history, and idempotency keys make 'who shipped what, where, when' recoverable.
SLO/error-budget gates drive a rollout state machine that auto-rolls-back degraded canaries.

say: Clarify the targets: ~40 microservices + a web frontend (deploy on demand) and 2 mobile apps (store-gated, slow cadence).
State the hardest constraints: fast PR feedback on a monorepo, and safe-by-default releases with rollback.
Start with the one-box script, name its failures, then introduce a DAG orchestrator on ephemeral runners.
Add affected-only builds + caching to hit the latency target.
Build-once immutable signed artifacts; promote the same digest.
Canary/blue-green via GitOps, then close the loop with observability-driven auto-rollback.
Contrast mobile: store review, staged rollout, no instant rollback — feature flags as the kill switch.

expectations: Knows artifacts should be built once and stored.
DAG + ephemeral runners + caching for speed.
Canary or blue-green with manual rollback.
Affected-target graph for monorepo speed; cache correctness + poisoning guards.
Immutable signed artifacts + provenance + SBOM/scan; deploy-time admission (build-once-promote).
Treats the platform as a state machine: run/stage/job/attempt state, idempotency, durable approvals, environment history, audit.
Observability-gated rollout state machine with auto-rollback; expand/contract migrations.
Articulates why mobile release mechanics differ (async tracks, review, crash gates, version skew) and uses flags as the kill switch.

followUps: How do you keep PR latency low as the monorepo grows 10x — remote build execution, test selection, merge queues?
How do you safely coordinate a feature that spans a backend change, a web change, and a mobile release?
How do you handle a database schema migration inside a canary rollout?
How do you secure the supply chain end-to-end (signed commits, SBOM, attestations, registry admission)?
How do you handle flaky tests so they don't block or falsely pass the pipeline?
